data.title: EU eastern states agree on need for 'drone wall', bloc's defence chief says
data.description:
Countries on the European Union's eastern flank have agreed on the need for a "drone wall" with advanced detection, tracking and interception capabilities, EU Defence Commissioner Andrius Kubilius said on Friday.

Drones now play a major role in the war in Ukraine, with both Moscow and Kyiv boosting their domestic production, while increased drone activity has exacerbated security concerns in several EU member states in recent weeks.

"Russia is testing the EU and NATO, and our response must be firm, united, and immediate," Kubilius told reporters in Finland after a videoconference with defence ministers mostly from the EU's eastern flank and representatives of Ukraine and NATO.

Participants have agreed to move from "discussions to concrete actions" and that the planned "drone wall" will be part of a broader Eastern Flank Watch that will also include ground defences and maritime security, he said.

"We shall look how to build a comprehensive European Union industrial policy and financial toolbox to make the shield a reality," he told a press conference.

Kubilius gave no details on funding but an EU official told Reuters that EU heads of state and government were expected to hold "intensive" discussions on the issue in October.

Representatives from Bulgaria, Estonia, Finland, Latvia, Lithuania, Poland, Romania, Hungary, Slovakia and Denmark attended Friday's video conference.

Kubilius stressed the need to develop additional capabilities and said a detailed technical roadmap would be drawn up with national experts.

Establishing a network of detectors must be a first priority, the commissioner said, adding that surveillance systems should be integrated and capabilities in areas such as jamming and drone interception improved.

Around 20 Russian drones entered Polish airspace on September 10, prompting NATO jets to shoot some of them down and the alliance to beef up defences along Europe's eastern flank.

This week drone incursions forced several airports, both civilian and military, to shut down temporarily in various parts of Denmark. Its defence minister branded the drone sorties as hybrid attacks but it remains unclear who was behind them.

Russia denied any involvement in the Danish incursions.

data.title: Nymex Overview: Petroleum Futures Rally; On Track for Weekly Gains — OPIS
data.description:
Crude oil and refined product futures prices were higher Friday through the morning and appear to be on track to lock in week-to-week gains.

Oil prices were up more than $1 heading into midday and Brent moved back above the $70 mark, with October RBOB rallying past the highs of when the contract first took the front-month position in early September.

While a lot of the buying has been related to issues with Russia and Ukraine's continued attacks on Russian refineries, trade sources said that there could be some capital rotation into oil as prices are considered cheap and volatility is relatively low. While the market is breaking out of the range that has been in place for much of the past six weeks, for the short-term it may be getting a bit "toppy," sources said.

Contracts were trading near the high end of the range with midday approaching. November West Texas Intermediate traded up to $66.42/bbl with prices near levels not seen since Aug. 4 and currently trading at $66.33/bbl, up $1.35. November Brent futures were up a similar amount and trading at $70.69/bbl, up $1.27 after peaking at $70.76 earlier.

After a move of more than 13cts over the past three trading days, the diesel contract is lagging the rest of the market, though it is still up on the day. Some profit-taking on the big crack spreads in the ULSD contract is likely keeping a lid on the contract's upside. Currently October and November ULSD are trading about 1.5cts higher. October ULSD is trading at $2.4418/gal with November at $2.4318/gal, up 1.58cts.

RBOB futures were moving along with crude oil, with October and November up more than 4.5cts, and that has boosted spot market prices.

October RBOB was at $2.0473/gal, up 4.51cts. A settlement at this level would represent the highest settlement price of the month as the contract expires next week. The November contract moved above the $2 level and was seeing a stronger move higher that was chewing into some of the backwardation. Currently the November contract is at $2.0034/gal, a 4.87ct gain.

Most spot markets are rading against November, and premiums on the West Coast are strong with Los Angeles up to a 52ct premium. Although Gulf Coast CBOB is at a discount to futures, the discount has narrowed to 4cts as spot prices are up more than 6cts.

data.title: Dow Industrials, S&P 500 Rise; Trump Unveils New Slate of Tariffs — WSJ
data.description:
By Joe Wallace and Vicky Ge Huang

President Trump unveiled new tariffs on drugs, trucks and furniture. U.S. stocks were mostly higher, putting the Dow on track to break a three-day losing streak.

There are two carveouts for the most significant of the tariffs, on pharmaceutical imports. Trump said the 100% levy would apply only to patented or branded drugs, and to those sold by companies that aren't building plants in America.

Shares of U.S. drug makers Eli Lilly, Merck and Pfizer rose. Several companies have committed to construction in America in recent months, and many large firms already have some production there. Plus, 90% of U.S. consumption by volume is of generic drugs.

Shares of German manufacturer Daimler Truck were down around 2%. U.S. stocks in tariffed sectors opened higher before pulling back. Wayfair was up about 1% and RH was down 4%.

The tariffs, which include 50% duties on kitchen cabinets, 30% on upholstered furniture and 25% on heavy trucks, are due to take effect on Wednesday.

U.S. truck builder Paccar, deemed a likely beneficiary of tariffs on overseas rivals, rose 5%.

Separately, the administration is weighing a plan to slice the U.S.'s reliance on semiconductors made overseas. The goal is to have companies manufacture the same number of semiconductors in the U.S. as their customers import from overseas. Intel shares rose.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0748 ET - The recent surge in Warner Bros. Discovery doesn't account for the fact that a buyout bid from Paramount Skydance may never materialize, say KeyBanc analysts Brandon Nispel and Matt Sbriglio, who downgrade the stock to sector weight. Warner Bros. Discovery shares have risen to nearly $20 from $12.54 on reports of a potential takeover offer from Paramount Skydance, but if a deal doesn't happen, shares would likely return to their original levels, the analysts speculate. "The stock's valuation has gotten ahead of the fundamentals," the analysts say. "We have no way to know if a deal will ultimately materialize." Warner Bros. Discovery is off 1.5% to $19.47 premarket. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0547 ET - Taiwan's decision to restrict chip exports to South Africa, which has since been paused, could alarm other countries, economists at Capital Economics say in a note. Taiwan suspended the restriction on Thursday, two days after the initial order, as the two sides agreed to discuss a dispute regarding Taiwan's representative office in the African country. "The threat raises questions about whether such curbs should now be considered part of Taiwan's armory in managing diplomatic ties," they say. Taiwan's dominant role in the semiconductor supply chain gives it some negotiating power, they say. Although Taiwan has paused the export ban, the perception that Taiwan might take advantage of its advanced chips for diplomatic goals could urge countries to reduce their dependence on Taiwanese chips in the future, the economists add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0515 ET - DBS Group Research turns bullish on Baidu, citing strong growth potential in its cloud and robotaxi businesses. The analysts expect Baidu's robotaxi unit to expand rapidly, noting its Wuhan operations have reached operating breakeven on a unit basis. They forecast its cloud business revenue to see on-year growth of 27% in 2025 and 20% in 2026, supported by strong AI demand. Meanwhile, its advertising business is undergoing an AI-driven revamp and could stabilize in 4Q. DBS upgrades Baidu to buy from hold, raising its ADR target to US$177.00 from US$85.00 and its Hong Kong target to HK$175.00 from HK$84.00. Baidu's ADRs closed at US$135.40, and its Hong Kong-listed shares ended at HK$129.10. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0323 ET - Astro Malaysia's operating environment remains challenging, Affin Hwang IB analysts write in a note. The satellite TV operator's introduction of "Astro One" bundles has slowed subscriber churn, among factors, signaling some early success in product bundling. However, the average revenue per user continues to fall at a faster pace, raising concerns over the sustainability of revenue and profitability even if subscriber losses moderate, the analysts say. Astro's radio and television advertising expenditure also remains soft. This suggests muted consumer and business sentiment, as U.S. tariffs and global trade uncertainties weigh on spending, the analysts say. Affin Hwang IB cuts Astro's target price to MYR0.08 from MYR0.13, while maintaining a sell rating on the stock. Shares areflat at MYR0.14.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0123 ET - Inari Amertron's earnings outlook seems more positive to MBSB Research as its radio frequency segment utilization rate rises following new smartphone launches, which supports steady revenue. The recent joint acquisition of Lumiled could diversify the company's earnings and tap into global LED and MicroLED growth, although regulatory approval is pending, it says in a note. The company's efforts to expand advanced packaging solutions could accelerate with government fiscal support. However, significant results are likely to be seen in FY2027 rather than FY2026, it adds. MBSB raises Inari's target price to MYR2.12 from MYR1.98, while maintaining a neutral rating on the stock. Shares are 1.4% lower at MYR2.12. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0048 ET - Life360's bulls at Morgan Stanley don't think that the location-app developer's price fully reflects the prospects for its new pet-tracking service. The MS analysts see potential upside in subscriber growth, retention and revenue from the bundling of the service to 11 million dog-owning families among Life360's user base. They also think that Life360 could launch new partnerships with third parties that increase the utility of its platform. They say in a note that they are looking forward to the dual-listed company's 3Q results in November and news on early take-up of the pet service. MS lifts its target price on Life360's Australia-listed stock 15% to A$58.50 and stays overweight. Shares are up 3.7% at A$52.87. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0041 ET - UBTech Robotics has a bright outlook with rising capacity and sales momentum, Citi analysts say in a note. The company will boost its humanoid robot capacity to about 300 units a month by end-September, enough to support 2026 deliveries, the analysts say, citing management. UBTech expects to deliver more than 500 units in 2025 and 2,000-3,000 units in 2026, backed by strong intent orders across industries. Citi says the company has a first-mover advantage in industrial applications. Citi lifts its 2025 and 2026 revenue forecasts by 5% and 3%, upgrades the stock to buy from buy/high risk and raises its target price to HK$170.00 from HK$121.00. Shares last traded at HK$139.30. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2325 ET - Tencent is likely to benefit from the recent surge in game approvals by Chinese authorities, Morningstar analyst Ivan Su write in a note. Su forecasts Tencent's revenue to grow 13.1% in 2025 and 10.4% in 2026 and adjusted EPS to rise 20.1% this year. The analyst notes that Chinese authorities approved 463 game licenses in 3Q, a 34% on-year increase and a 23% year-to-date rise through September, suggesting the Chinese gaming industry is on track for double-digit growth in the coming quarters. Morningstar views Tencent as undervalued, trading at a 20% discount to its valuation, maintaining its fair value estimates of $102.00 on Tencent's ADR, which last closed at $83.31. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

2310 ET - TSMC's 2025 revenue could beat its already lifted full-year guidance of 30% growth in U.S. dollar terms amid rising AI demand, Citi analysts say in a research note. TSMC could see even stronger demand for its advanced chips into 2027 thanks to the bigger integrated-circuit size for the next-generation chips and expected strong 2-nanometer chip ramp-up starting from 2H 2026, they say. TSMC's capex could reach US$47 billion and US$50 billion in 2026 and 2027, respectively, they say. Citi thinks TSMC will like reallocate more of its research and design resources and capacity to the most advanced nodes and advanced packaging. Maintaining a buy call on TSMC, Citi raises its target price to NT$1,600.00 from NT$1,400.00. Shares were recently at NT$1,300.00. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2236 ET - Montage Technology is likely capitalizing on the memory migration cycle, Daiwa Capital Markets analysts say in a research report. It has strong competitiveness in the global memory interconnect chip market, with an improving product mix amid the migration to the DDR5 memory technology standard from DDR4. Its interconnect chip business is likely to grow over the next few years, with the technology migration requiring higher-value memory interconnect chips. It's also developing interconnect solutions related to Serializer-Deserializer high-speed serial interface technology for growth, they note. Daiwa initiates coverage of the stock with a buy rating and a target price of CNY165.00. Shares 0.6% higher at CNY137.54. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2104 ET - Astro Malaysia's earnings outlook may remain weak given subdued consumer sentiment, Maybank IB analysts Nur Natasha Ariza and Yin Shao Yang say in a note. Although Astro expects advertisers' sentiment to improve as tariff uncertainties ease, the analysts are less optimistic. They highlight that the electricity tariff hike effective July and the lower RON95 subsidized fuel price are unlikely to provide a meaningful boost to ad demand. They cut Astro's FY2026-FY2028 core net profit forecasts by 39%-48%, as recent earnings came in below estimates. Maybank cuts Astro's target price to MYR0.07 from MYR0.12, while maintaining a sell rating on the stock. Shares are unchanged at MYR0.14. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1613 ET - Reddit has a pipeline of new revenue opportunities at its disposal as it looks to close its monetization gap, Baird analysts say. The company's search business has a roughly $400 billion dollar domestic market opportunity, with a text-oriented app expected to contribute monetization next year, they say. Shopping ads are also a key medium-term opportunity as Reddit launches performance ads with measurable return on investment in the coming year, they say. The platform is also gaining traction internationally through machine translation, they add. Those opportunities, among others, continue to provide upside potential, they say, raising their price target on the stock to $240 from $177. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0840 ET - New U.S. drug tariffs came with an exception for companies building new factories in the U.S., but short-term levies could still be faced if projects aren't under way by Oct. 1, Panmure Liberum analysts write. Britain's GSK and AstraZeneca have announced major investments in the U.S., which might shield them, they write. However, those programs won't launch until 2026, a deadline that may expose the groups to short-term levies, the analysts say. Meanwhile, the announcement was encouraging for phrama groups that focus on generic drugs, as they appear to be exempt from tariff plans, they add. Hikma and Sandoz shares are up 1.7% and 1.3%, respectively, while GSK and AstraZeneca are steady. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0813 ET - New U.S. drug tariffs shouldn't affect Belgian pharma group UCB as the company outlined plans to build a manufacturing site in the country, ING analysts write. ING says that, as far as it is aware, the 15% ceiling on U.S. levies on imports from the EU applies to branded pharmaceuticals under the recent U.S.-EU tariff deal, the analysts say. Moreover, 65% of UCB's products are manufactured by third parties worldwide, they add. UCB said in June that it would build a manufacturing plant in the U.S. that would create 300 permanent and 500 construction jobs. UCB shares rise 0.8% and are up 3.1% year-on-year. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0757 ET - It is unclear how new U.S. drug tariffs relate to a U.S.-EU trade deal capping levies, Novo Nordisk CEO Mike Doustdar says. The Danish pharma group looks forward to working with the Trump administration, but it is uncertain whether the earlier deal between the U.S. and the EU will keep tariffs at 15% rather than the 100% announced by President Trump, Doustdar writes in a statement. Construction is under way on a Novo Nordisk manufacturing site in North Carolina, he adds. Trump announced Thursday that the tariffs would hit pharmaceutical companies not building U.S. plants. Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.7%. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0707 ET - A German business group representing chemical and pharmaceutical companies is urging the EU to push for an agreement with President Trump on tariffs for medicines. VCI says it is very concerned about Trump's plan to impose an 100% tariff on pharmaceutical imports as of October. The move comes after the U.S. and the EU struck a deal this summer for a 15% U.S. tariff on medicines coming from the bloc. VCI CEO Wolfgang Grosse Entrup says the tariff announcement is a low point for EU-U.S. trade relations. The U.S. is an important market for the German pharmaceutical industry, with exports amounting to 27.9 billion euros in 2024, he adds. The Stoxx Europe 600 Health Care index is up 0.1%. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0409 ET - U.S. President Trump's new round of sectoral tariff are likely to negatively affect highly trade-dependent economies like Singapore, DBS senior economist Chua Han Teng writes in an email. The measures, including a 100% tariff on branded or patented drug imports from Oct. 1, marks a continuation of Trump's protectionist agenda, he says. "Continued imposition of U.S. tariffs and ongoing uncertainty of new ones will hurt already weakened business sentiment," Chua says. He notes, however, the exemption for drugmakers building a production facility in the U.S. may mitigate the blow. Singapore hosts nine of the world's top 10 drugmakers, with major pharma firms already establishing or expanding manufacturing operations in the U.S., Chua notes.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0405 ET - President Trump's drug tariffs will have a limited impact on big Swiss pharma players given their U.S. presence, Vontobel analysts write. While there is broad room for interpretation of Trump's announcement, Roche and Novartis are shielded by their U.S. footprints, while Sandoz should be unaffected since it focuses on exempted generic products, the analysts say. Galderma's exposure is less clear as it has a smaller U.S. presence. Tariffs will increase U.S. drug prices and supply concerns, but they shouldn't threaten the country's leading position in drug development, Vontobel says. Galderma shares fall 3.2%, with Roche and Novartis down 0.3% and 0.2%, respectively. Sandoz shares trade broadly flat. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0337 ET - European pharmaceutical stocks fell in early trade after President Trump announced 100% tariffs on those companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. Swiss skincare company Galderma was the biggest faller, losing 3.55%, while Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.3%. AstraZeneca, Roche and Bayer shares were all down 0.2%. Tariffs are expected to start from Oct. 1.(ian.walker@wsj.com)

0239 ET - The impact of U.S. medical-device tariffs on European med-tech companies hinges on whether products for disabled people will be exempted, Bernstein analysts write. Products for disabled people are exempted from tariffs under the Nairobi Protocol and the effect of tariffs on the sector will depend on whether levies can override it, according to Bernstein. If applicable, chronic-care specialists including Coloplast and ConvaTec would be more exposed to potential levies than hearing-aid makers including Sonova, Demant, GN Store Nord and Amplifon, the analysts say. Another critical factor is manufacturing location. A recent U.S.-EU trade agreement caps tariffs on medicines at 15%, but it is unclear if similar agreements would shield medical technologies, Bernstein writes. Convatec fell 5.6%, Demant 4.8%, and GN 6.6% on Thursday. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0041 ET - The U.S.'s fresh tariffs on pharmaceutical imports aren't a big concern for China's healthcare sector, according to Jefferies analysts in a research note. Chinese pharmaceutical companies have minimal sales exposure to the U.S. market, the analysts say. Investors may have concerns regarding Chinese biotechs' U.S. expansion, but at this stage, most companies are at least 1-2 years away from potential U.S. commercialization, they note. Also, most Chinese biotechs have U.S. partners and are immune to the tariffs, so they barely dent China's healthcare powerhouses, the analysts add. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

2232 ET - IHH Healthcare is expected to post steady 2H earnings before stronger growth in 2026 on strength in its key regions, Affin Hwang IB analyst Andrew Lim says in a note. Singapore continues to be IHH's biggest earnings driver, with growth in 2026 supported by Mount Elizabeth Hospital reopening its beds. Payor pressures in Malaysia may cap margins, but India is set to deliver the fastest growth, supported by Fortis's bright prospects and aggressive five-year bed expansion plan, he says. Lim is positive on IHH's outlook amid rising inpatient demand, stable margins and a demand-driven expansion, supported by growing healthcare awareness. Affin Hwang maintains a buy rating on IHH and keeps its target price at MYR8.10. Shares are 0.8% higher at MYR7.43. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2141 ET - Malaysia's healthcare sector is poised for sustained long-term growth, driven by rising noncommunicable diseases and an aging population, CIMB Securities analyst Walter Aw Lik Hsin says in a note. Private hospital bed capacity is expected to grow 4%-5% annually through 2028, narrowing the gap with developed markets, he says. A government campaign to attract foreign travelers is set to boost healthcare tourism, reinforcing long-term sector growth, he adds. The sector's near-term challenges include manpower shortages and regulatory uncertainty, but private operators like IHH Healthcare and KPJ Healthcare are better positioned to attract talent. CIMB maintains an overweight rating on the sector, with IHH Healthcare as its top pick due to its diversified assets. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1934 ET [Dow Jones]--Volatility in Telix Pharmaceuticals's share price is set to continue — this time for positive reasons. That's the view of Bell Potter after Telix secured transitional pass through, or TPT, status for its Gozellix imaging agent for prostate cancer in the U.S. "The decision to pursue this strategy (for a refresh on the pass through) has proven to be a master stroke by the company and one that has shown its major competitor in the PSMA imaging market asleep at the wheel," analyst John Hester says. TPT status on Gozellix is effective Oct. 1. Bell Potter assumes an average reimbursement of $5,000 per patient. It retains a buy call on Telix and A$23.00/share price target. Telix ended Thursday at A$15.38. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0850 ET - Switzerland plans to give UBS Group seven years to comply with tougher capital requirements, as the country's government detailed the timing of implementation of an update to its "too big to fail" regulatory regime. "The government points out that the 7-year period is depending on the parliamentary progress which may suggest that the end date is fixed," RBC Capital Markets says in a note. The Swiss Federal Council on Friday said a requirement to increase UBS's capital buffer will start in 2028 and be gradually implemented until the bank can fully back its foreign subsidiaries by the end of 2034. The government launched a consultation on the plan that is due to end on Jan. 9, 2026. UBS shares gain 0.2%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0341 ET - Moshi Moshi Retail is expected to maintain its market leadership despite rising competition, supported by differentiated products, efficient sourcing and a large store network, Maybank Securities says. Analyst Suttatip Peerasub projects gross margin improvement in 2025-2026 from higher-margin products and better supplier terms as production scales. Maybank forecasts 3Q net profit growth of 13% and sales growth of 15% on solid same-store sales and store additions, with full-year earnings projected to rise 19% in 2025 and 16% in 2026. Moshi also deserves a premium valuation given its three-year EPS CAGR of 16.7%, more than double the sector average of 7.6%, Peerasub adds. Maybank maintains a buy rating and THB51.00 target on Moshi's stock, which was recently at THB40.75.(jason.chau@wsj.com)

0040 ET - Morgan Stanley analysts think that some observers are missing the size of the likely second-half skew in Macquarie's earnings. Their forecast for 6% first-half earnings growth significantly lags behind consensus, but they think that analysts elsewhere aren't seeing the full picture. They say in a note that a recent client compensation agreement and risks of green-energy asset write-downs could also weigh on profit. However, they think that a recovery in global capital markets and commodities revenues should drive a powerful second-half recovery. They are looking for 12% earnings growth across the full year, and their annual net profit forecast is broadly in line with consensus. MS stays equal-weight on the stock and lifts its target price by 4.6% to A$226.00. Shares are up 0.4% at A$216.82. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1912 ET [Dow Jones]--Fonterra Shareholders' Fund sets a high bar for future earnings, prompting Macquarie to raise its price target on the dairy cooperative by 17% to NZ$8.61/share. Fonterra has agreed to the sale of its consumer businesses, known as Mainland Group, to Lactalis for NZ$3.85 billion. It expects to take three years to replace the hit to operating profit from that deal. Fonterra is targeting 5% annual Ebit growth to FY 2028, the bank says. Its new price target reflects the Mainland sale and higher sustainable earnings implied in the Ebit target, although it is less bullish about this goal than management. Fonterra is up 1.3% at NZ$7.91 today. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1011 ET - Oil futures are higher on rising geopolitical risk premium with continuing Ukraine attacks on Russian oil facilities,and President Trump's rekindling of tariff battles with duties on pharmaceuticals and heavy trucks. Although most of the buying has been by short-sided traders liquidating their positions, "the crude market has gained merit on steady demand seemingly keeping up with added supplies from OPEC+," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. The latest tariffs may signal to India and China "that additional tariffs could be heading their way if they remain buyers of Russian crude," he adds.WTI is up 0.8% at $65.48 a barrel and Brent gains 0.6% to $69.83. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0911 ET - Canada GDP is on track on for 0.8% annualized growth in 3Q following the release of July GDP data, says Andrew Grantham, economist at CIBC Capital Markets. GDP by industry climbed 0.2% in July from the prior month, powered by Canada's oil-and-gas sector, marking the first month-over-month increase since March. But the Statistics Canada says early indicators suggest GDP was flat in August, which "suggests that the economy stalled again that month," Grantham says. The Bank of Canada forecast a 1% annualized gain for 3Q. Grantham says the BOC will likely lean on the pending employment and inflation data before deciding on whether to cut rates again on October 29. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0332 ET - China's new 2035 target of 3,600GW of solar and wind power capacity looks conservative and will likely be exceeded, Deutsche Bank analyst Gary Zhou says. He expects supply-side reform to remain the near-term focus, as restoring supply-demand balance in the renewable sector is key for investors. On the demand side, Zhou forecasts China will add 1,455GW of solar and 600GW of wind power capacities during the 2026-2030 period, well above additions in 2021-2025. DB's top renewables pick is GCL Tech, citing its low valuation and potential gains from solar supply reforms. Shares last traded at HK$1.27. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

1917 ET [Dow Jones]--Carnarvon Energy now owns 18-19% of Strike Energy's equity following the latter's capital raising, effectively securing a blocking stake in the Western Australia-focused natural- gas producer. That means Carnarvon "is now integral in any commercial negotiations if Hancock was seeking to fully consolidate the Perth basin Kingia acreage," says Macquarie. Hancock bought 50% of the West Erregulla natural-gas field in 2023 for A$450 million. Strike Energy owns the other 50% of West Erregulla. Macquarie resumes coverage of Strike Energy with a neutral call and A$0.10/share price target. Strike Energy ended Thursday at A$0.105. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1519 ET - Crude futures recover early losses and end the session mixed with continued concerns over Russian supplies keeping sellers cautious. "There's no doubt that right now that the Russian hits on their oil infrastructure by Ukraine means that that really is Ground Zero," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. With Europe planning tougher sanctions on Russian oil and gas, it "could create a situation where we could start to see significant change in market psychology and a potential breakout to the upside." WTI slips 1 cent to $64.98 a barrel, and Brent settles up 0.2% at $69.42. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1350 ET - Oil futures are pulling up from earlier lows with continued support from tensions over the Russia-Ukraine war. Yesterday's WTI break above the 200-day moving average was "a technical signal that suggests a bullish market trend," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. WTI briefly retested the 200-day moving average as support, "and successfully held the level." he adds. WTI is off 0.1% at $64.91 a barrel and Brent is near flat at $69.29. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1035 ET - Silver futures are pushing towards highs last seen in April 2011, according to data from FactSet. The most-active contract is now up 1.8% to $45.91 a troy ounce. While gold has dominated headlines, analysts are also looking at strong gains elsewhere. "It has been silver and platinum that have delivered the most eye-catching returns," says Ole Hansen of Saxo Bank in a note. Industrial demand has been a factor underpinning strength in silver, he adds, along with investors seeking the metal as an alternative to gold. "The metal's high-beta nature has amplified inflows from investors seeking leverage to gold's record-breaking run," says Hansen. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0232 ET - Lotte Chemical's earnings could start to recover in 2028 after completing its business restructuring, KB Securities analysts Wooje Chun and Yunju Song write in a note. The South Korean company is still reeling from the worst-ever global chemical-industry downturn, and is expected to complete its ongoing business consolidation by 2027, they say. The analysts note that global naphtha cracking center operators in Europe, South Korea, China and Japan shut down some of their facilities for four years until 2027 to address the oversupply. KB expects Lotte Chemical's operating loss to narrow to KRW287.5 billion in 2026. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0053 ET - The price of copper "will need to rise dramatically" to spur enough investment in new mines to meet demand, Shaw and Partners analysts say in a note. Bringing on new copper supply is challenging and production costs are likely to rise, say the analysts. "Expansions of existing mines are becoming increasingly expensive, and new projects often involve lower-grade ore and higher development costs," they say. "This is compounded by the slow pace of new discoveries and stricter environmental and social standards, creating a bottleneck that prevents a rapid increase in supply to meet growing demand." Shaw expects copper to average $10,524/metric ton next year, versus consensus of $9,786/ton and spot of $10,287/ton. The broker's key copper stock picks are AIC Mines, Aurelia Metals and Sunstone Metals. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2212 ET - Liontown Resources provides some additional disclosures on its FY 2025 results call, including that it expects capital expenditure of A$55 million-A$70 million from FY 2028 onward, Citi analyst Kate McCutcheon says in a note. That compares to Citi's expectation of roughly A$80 million a year, says McCutcheon. Liontown also flags flat depreciation and amortization in FY 2026 year over year, before a moderation in FY 2027, she adds. Liontown's FY 2025 revenue is in line with expectations, and Citi makes only minor changes to its estimates following the results, McCutcheon says. Citi has a sell rating on Liontown. It raises its target to A$0.50 from A$0.45. Liontown is up 6.1% at A$0.96. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2128 ET - Geo Energy Resources stands to benefit from the construction of a 92-kilometer hauling road and a jetty in Indonesia's South Sumatra, Phillip Securities Research's Paul Chew says in commentary. There is no change to the projected completion and commissioning by mid-2026, with an additional two months needed to become fully operational. Once completed, coal production at the Singapore-listed Indonesian company's TRA mine in South Sumatra is expected to increase to 12 million metric tons in 2027 from 2 million metric tons in 2025. The brokerage raises its target price on the stock to S$0.59 from S$0.47 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 1.1% higher at S$0.455. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2120 ET - Growth in the rare-earth supply chain outside of China depends on manufacturers and governments being receptive to higher prices for rare earths produced elsewhere, Morgan Stanley analyst Rahul Anand says in a note. Anand's remarks follow a Reuters report that says G-7 nations and the European Union are considering price floors to encourage production. Already, the U.S. has set a price floor for some light rare earths in a deal with MP Materials. Australian officials say they are also considering price floors for critical minerals. MS remains overweight on Australia's Lynas and Iluka "as key beneficiaries of higher ex-China rare earths prices," says Anand. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1427 ET - Gold futures settle slightly higher on strong physical metal flow. Gold ETFs have seen inflows totaling $10.5 billion in September, says Citi Research in a note. Year-to-date, the flow to ETFs is roughly $50 billion. "ETF has outshined all other gold demand sectors this year and is the single most important contributor to the gold price rally in our view," says Kenny Hu of Citi Research. Front-month gold futures close up 0.1% to $3,736.90 a troy ounce, for the third-highest finish on record. Today marks the fourth positive session in the past five for gold. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1346 ET - Worthington Steel's hot-rolled coil pricing is pressured by softer volumes in most of its end markets, resulting in wider holding losses, Chief Financial Officer Tim Adams tells analysts. Current pricing is about $800 per ton, down from a peak of $950 in March, he says. The steel processor expects to generate inventory holding losses in its F2Q of $5 million to $10 million, compared with the $5.6 million in the current quarter, Adams says. The lower prices come as tariffs on imported steel increased in June. Worthington Steel slides 7% to $31.54. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1316 ET - Worthington Steel's automotive market is finally making a comeback, Chief Financial Officer Tim Adams says during an analyst call. The sector was one of the few bright spots during F1Q, as shipments increased 17% from the prior year. The auto market itself is improving and the company now expects to finish the year with a 15 million unit build rate, up from prior forecasts of 13.5 million. Worthington also anticipates tailwinds from potential interest rate cuts. Beyond improving industry trends, Worthington has also won market share by ramping up volumes and new programs, executives say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1301 ET - Freeport-McMoRan's stock offers an attractive buying opportunity as share falls on lowered production forecasts after a mudslide at one of its mines, Jefferies analysts say in a note. While the mudslide is a clear negative for the company, some of this downside was already reflected in the share price because the market had been expecting a downgrade to guidance, the analysts say. The disruption should also lead to a tighter copper market and a higher copper price, which would be positive for the company's operations in the Americas. "We would buy FCX on today's weakness," they say. The stock falls 5%, to $35.78, extending its week-to-date decline to 21%.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1252 ET - Worthington Steel is facing soft demand due to macroeconomic uncertainty, Chief Financial Officer Tim Adams tells analysts. Agriculture is the most challenged market, as volumes were down 50% year-over-year due to continued softness in the agricultural equipment sector, Adams says. Shipments to the heavy truck market fell 7% and to construction customers fell 3%. Toll processing volumes fell 22% because of less processing from mills and service centers, as well as the closure of a processing plant in Ohio, Adams says. "We experienced pluses and minuses across various markets as customers continued to navigate uncertainty during the quarter," he said. Shares fall 6.4%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1239 ET - Freeport-McMoRan 2027 production levels could also be at risk after the company cut its guidance for this year and next year after a fatal mudslide at one of its mines, Jefferies analysts say in a note. Management now expects negligible sales volumes at the mine in the fourth quarter and is guiding to as much as 35% lower volumes in 2026 compared to its prior forecast, the analysts say. "There is downside risk to 2027 production as well, in our view," they say, noting the Grasberg mine is one of the largest and lowest cost mines in the world, accounting for about 3% of global mined copper supply. They lower their price target on the stock to $46 from $48. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1257 GMT - CarMax seems to be investing more on lowering prices but that hasn't led to a recovery in market share or same-store sales, which is a worrying sign, say JPMorgan analysts, who cut the stock's price target to $50 from $65. It suggests a slippery slope that could be hard to recover from and that pressures both gross profit per unit and volumes. Over the last five years, CarMax has made significant investments in omni-channel and sourcing functions but has been unable to gain share. "The business has not been agile enough to adjust to volatile industry backdrops despite prior experiences," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1251 GMT - Truck customers are likely to wait and see how new U.S. tariffs influence pricing, so expect orders and demand to take a hit in the near term, Jefferies analysts write. If prices increase across the industry in response, demand will likely be ht in the fourth quarter and into 2026, they add. Trump plans to impose 25% tariffs on heavy-duty trucks, but it isn't known how vehicles with parts made in the U.S., or which are USMCA-compliant, will be treated. Jefferies estimates that Daimler Truck and Traton could be at a 300-600 basis point cost disadvantage to manufacturers that assemble most vehicles in the U.S., such as Volvo and Paccar. Daimler Truck shares fall 2.3%, Traton shares are down 2% while Volvo shares rise 3.4%. Paccar shares rise 6.7% pre-market. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1232 GMT - - President Trump's new tariffs on trucks put around 18% of Daimler Truck's 2026 group profit at risk and 9% of Traton's, according to Bank of America Securities estimates. Daimler Truck currently manufactures around 60% of its U.S. sales in Mexico, with Traton at 65%, making them particularly vulnerable, analysts at the bank say. In practice, the bank expects both companies to offset tariffs by moving some production into the U.S. and raising prices, with 6% price increase probably enough to fully offset the impact. By contrast, 100% of Volvo's North American truck production is within the U.S. "We think this positions the company well to boost margins and/or market share." Daimler Truck shares fall 2.5% and Traton shares are down 2% while Volvo shares rise 3.7%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1230 GMT - Despite a sizable 2Q earnings miss, CarMax's business model remains solid, but its recovery will take time amid increasing competition and weaker demand, say Oppenheimer analysts, who downgrade the stock to perform. Repositioning efforts could be disruptive in the short-term, the analysts say, adding that investors are unlikely to bet on the stock without clear recovery signals. "We are now increasingly concerned that the company must endure a potentially elongated strategic retrenchment, focused upon restrengthening and marketing better core consumer propositions, all the while contending with a potentially weakening demand backdrop," the analysts say. CarMax is 1% lower premarket after sliding 20% Thursday. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1222 GMT - CarMax's 2Q across-the-board miss reveals that it's also losing market share, Truist says, cutting the price target to $47 from $74. Its competitor Carvana is growing rapidly and "has gained material mindshare with customers focused on an online experience," analyst Scot Ciccarelli says. Additionally, compared with CarMax, traditional dealers "have deeper inventory pools of specific makes/models, appealing to customers that have already narrowed-down their choices," he says. The company also continues to struggle with its price perception among consumers, according to Ciccarelli. CarMax is off 1% premarket after plunging 20% Thursday. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0822 GMT - Horizon Robotics' top-up share placement could be a slight surprise for investors, say DBS Group Research analysts in a note. The Chinese technology company is raising HK$6.38 billion via a placement. The analysts note the "relatively short" three months since a previous placement of around US$600 million and a rich net cash reserve of 15.5 billion yuan as of end-1H. Nonetheless, DBS thinks this move is reasonable for the company, which provides smart-driving solutions for vehicles, as the share price has risen 34% since its 1H results release and is hovering near an all-time high. DBS trims its target to HK$11.00 from HK$11.50 but maintains a buy rating on Horizon Robotics, which closed 8.5% lower at HK$9.70. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0748 GMT - Bernstein initiates coverage on Rheinmetall's stock with a market-perform rating and a target price of 1,960 euros. The bank believes that the German arms maker will continue to win orders in the coming years, as demand outstrips supply and early capacity increases drive the growth. "But after the first phase of replenishment and during the transition to long-cycle investments, we worry a perfect storm in short-cycle (armoured vehicles, shells) products." Bernstein says that a transition to a long-cycle portfolio will be long and costly. Analysts expect more attractive risk-rewards for players that are already positioned, at cheaper valuations. Bernstein's top pick is BAE Systems. Rheinmetall shares are down 0.3% at 1,972.50 euros. (alistair.macdonald@wsj.com)

0739 GMT - Safran will get a boost from Turkish Airlines' Boeing aircraft purchase, as the French manufacturer produces the Leap engine for the 737 MAX and equipment for the 787 plane, Jefferies analysts say. Turkish Airlines said Friday that it decided to purchase a total of 75 aircraft from Boeing, consisting of 50 firm and 25 option orders for 787-9 and 787-10 models with deliveries scheduled between 2029 and 2034. Negotiations with Boeing regarding the purchase of a total of 150 aircraft, consisting of 100 firm and 50 option orders for the 737-8/10 MAX models, have been completed, the carrier said. Safran shares are up 1.2% at 295.60 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0718 GMT - President Trump's announcement of 25% tariffs on imported heavy trucks is light on detail, but Daimler Truck could suffer the most given current production in the U.S. and overall exposure to the U.S. at the group level, Bernstein analysts write. U.S. manufacturer Paccar could be the greatest beneficiary, it adds. Bernstein notes the tariffs apply from Oct. 1, so they won't impact the third quarter, where orders and volumes are weak for all manufacturers. It adds that further inflationary increases to truck prices will negatively impact all, especially with new truck prices currently considerably elevated after many years of price increases. It rates Volvo and Daimler Truck at underperform and Traton at market-perform. Volvo shares rise 2.1%, Daimler Truck shares fall 4.4% and Traton shares drop 2.1%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0652 GMT - The Philippine port sector's outlook appears positive, Maybank Securities' Germaine Guinto says in a research report. The ports' container volumes gained strong momentum in 1H and are poised to quicken in 2H, the analyst says. Growth is anchored by resilient domestic consumption and a pickup in trade activity, with foreign containers accounting for the majority of the ports' traffic, the analyst says. International Container Terminal Services, via Manila International Container Terminal port's dominant gateway role, remains best-placed to capture this momentum, the analyst adds. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of PHP590.00 on International Container Terminal Services shares, which are 0.6% lower at PHP495.80. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1905 GMT - Electric vehicle sales in the U.S. are expected to hit a record of around 409,000 in the third quarter, as consumers take advantage of government-backed incentives slated to end Sept. 30, Cox Automotive said. The prior record was set in the fourth quarter of 2024, with 365,824 units sold, according to Cox. The share of EV sales in the third quarter will likely also be a record, coming in at close to 10% of total sales, Cox added. "The federal tax credit was a key catalyst for EV adoption, and its expiration marks a pivotal moment," said Stephanie Valdez Streaty, director of Industry Insights at Cox Automotive. "This shift will test whether the electric vehicle market is mature enough to thrive on its own fundamentals or still needs support to expand further." Sales of EVs are expected to slow in the fourth quarter, although long-term sales growth will continue, Cox said. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1856 GMT - Cox Automotive said the September new-vehicle sale pace is expected to be around 16.2 million, up from 15.8 million in September 2024 and higher than August's 16.1 million. Year over year, third-quarter sales volume is expected to rise 6.2%, reaching 4.14 million. "The new-vehicle sales pace has been surprisingly strong this summer and through the third quarter as uncertainty around tariff policy has decreased," said Charlie Chesbrough, senior economist at Cox Automotive. "Continued low inflation and unemployment rates, coupled with a strong stock market, have kept consumers in a buying mood," Chesbrough added. Sales numbers have been helped by an increase in EV sales, as buyers move to make purchases before tax credits expire at the end of September, Chesbrough said. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

data.title: German carmakers to meet Chancellor Merz at summit in October
data.description:
Executives of Germany's top carmakers will meet Chancellor Friedrich Merz at a summit planned for October 9 in Berlin, a person with direct knowledge of the matter said.

The meeting comes as Germany's giant auto industry grapples with U.S. tariffs, slumping sales, and warnings on profits.

The Chancellery in Berlin did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The source, who spoke on condition of anonymity, said Volkswagen , Mercedes-Benz and BMW would be among those companies represented.

Uncertainty for the sector grew on Friday after U.S. President Donald Trump announced tariffs on heavy-duty trucks, adding to trade tensions that have hit the automotive sector hard.

data.description:
RNS Number : 0825B Compagnie de Saint-Gobain 26 September 2025  

SAINT-GOBAIN has INAUGURATEd THE FIRST 100% RENEWABLE ELECTRICITY PLASTERBOARD PLANT IN NORTH AMERICA

Saint-Gobain has today inaugurated its CertainTeed plasterboard plant in Sainte-Catherine, near Montreal, Canada, which is now fully electrified and powered by hydroelectricity. This site becomes the first zero-carbon (scope 1 and 2) plasterboard plant in North America and the largest in the world to be fully powered by electricity.

The launch of a new production line at the existing plant, along with equipment modernization, has enabled a 40% increase in production capacity while reducing energy consumption by 30% and CO₂ emissions by approximately 44,000 tons per year.

"This ambitious project strengthens our leadership position in light construction in Canada and meets the strong local demand for sustainable solutions. It accelerates our growth in the country, where we have tripled in size in 3 years", explains Mark Rayfield, CEO Saint-Gobain North America.

This 100% renewable electricity plasterboard plant is the Group's second after the one in Fredrikstad, Norway, inaugurated in 2023. This initiative is a new clear manifestation of Saint-Gobain's commitment to achieving net zero carbon emissions by 2050. 

About Saint-Gobain

Worldwide leader in light and sustainable construction, Saint-Gobain designs, manufactures and distributes materials and services for the construction and industrial markets. Its integrated solutions for the renovation of public and private buildings, light construction and the decarbonization of construction and industry are developed through a continuous innovation process and provide sustainability and performance. The Group, celebrating its 360th anniversary in 2025, remains more committed than ever to its purpose "MAKING THE WORLD A BETTER HOME".

€46.6 billion in sales in 2024

More than 161,000 employees, locations in 80 countries

Committed to achieving net zero carbon emissions by 2050

data.title: The Most-Shorted Rally Might Lose Steam — WSJ
data.description:
By Spencer Jakab

This is an online version of my Markets A.M. newsletter. Get investing insights in your inbox each weekday by signing up here-it's free.

Short selling isn't for the faint of heart-especially lately. But a rally in speculative stocks that has given hedge funds fits might be ebbing.

Borrowing shares and then selling them, betting they can be bought back more cheaply, can be attractive at any time. The irony is that frothy periods produce more short-selling candidates, yet also can be the most dangerous times to engage in the practice.

Consider a hedge-fund manager who thinks Company A is solid but Company B is an overvalued basket case. He can borrow shares of B and sell them, using the cash from the sale to increase his bet on A.

But selling short opens his fund up to unlimited losses if B defies expectations and soars. Then he might be forced to quickly buy B back, adding to its gains.

Such short squeezes are more common when many individual speculators are active and convinced they have a knack for trading. A money-losing company in a hot sector might attract such buyers and then start rising just because short sellers are forced to cover their bet.

The combination of social media and a boom in stock options has made squeezes more common and potent. Sometimes the reason for the crowd's excitement over a stock is the very fact that a high percentage of its shares have been sold short.

In January 2021, Melvin Capital lost half its assets when GameStop shares surged. Hedge funds have become more cautious, but heavily shorted stocks have risen steadily since April's Liberation Day swoon.

That rally faltered this week as doubts about AI profitability helped send stocks to their longest losing streak in a month.

"Tuesday feels like it was a very major peak in sentiment," says Peter Atwater, who studies public confidence and teaches at William & Mary.

If he's right then the experience could be particularly unpleasant for owners of shorted stocks.

Analysts at Leuthold Group note that, from that original meme-stock episode through June, there were five months when the 50 most-shorted stocks beat the market by 10 percentage points or more, on average. September was recently on track to do the same. After the original meme episode, the shorted stocks then trailed the market during 14 of the next 17 months by an average of 6 percentage points monthly.

Through Monday, a basket of volatile stocks belonging to the S&P 500 High Beta Index had returned 72% from their April low-double the S&P 500's rise. A "most-shorted" basket tracked by UBS and a "money-losing tech" group tracked by Goldman Sachs also soared.

"Coming into this week the rubber band was as stretched as it gets in some parts of the market," wrote strategists at BTIG. Stocks they highlighted as vulnerable include Palantir, Robinhood, Centrus Energy and QuantumScape.

This week's losses haven't been fun, but some funds will welcome a more rational market.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

Rule 8 of the Takeover Code (the "Code") sets out the circumstances in which Dealing Disclosures and/or Opening Position Disclosures are required to be made by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them, persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more and exempt principal traders ("EPTs") connected with the parties to an offer.

2.

Where an offeror is marked "Disclosure of dealings and positions in this offeror is not required", dealings and positions in relevant securities of that offeror are not required to be disclosed under Rule 8. This is either because it has been announced that the offer or possible offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash or because disclosure is not required for some other reason.

3.

Dealings and positions required to be disclosed under Rule 8 of the Code include dealings and positions in derivatives referenced to, and options in respect of, relevant securities of an offeree company and, where appropriate, an offeror (see Note 2 above).

4.

The information set out in the Disclosure Table relating to the relevant securities in issue of an offeree company or of an offeror (and any amendments thereto) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by such company under Rule 2.9 of the Code. Similarly, details of any deadline set under Rule 2.6 by which a potential offeror must announce either a firm intention to make a bid or that it does not intend to make an offer (or, in the case of Rule 2.6(a), by which the Panel must consent to an extension of the deadline) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by the offeree company.

5.

Additions and deletions of offeree companies and offerors are reflected in the Disclosure Table throughout the day, following the relevant announcement. Other amendments may also be made throughout the day. In any event, details of the classes of relevant securities issued by an offeree or offeror company, the number of such securities in issue ("NSI") and the International Securities Identification Number ("ISIN") of such securities announced under Rule 2.9 of the Code before 5.00 pm each day will be included in the final version of the Disclosure Table by 5.30 pm that day. The final version of the Disclosure Table issued each day will be marked "Final" and persons required to make Dealing Disclosures and Opening Position Disclosures are advised to use that final version. Any changes to the Rule 2.6 deadline of a potential offeror will also be reflected in the "Final" version of the Disclosure Table, although any such changes will not be shown in the "Amendments" section at the top of the Disclosure Table.

6.

Dealing Disclosures by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them and connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the business day (as defined in the Code) following the date of the relevant dealing and Dealing Disclosures by persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by 3.30 pm on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

7.

Opening Position Disclosures by the parties to an offer and by connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Where required, Opening Position Disclosures by persons with interests in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by no later than 3.30 pm on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Details of the time and date at which an offer period commenced and at which an offeror was first identified are set out in the Disclosure Table. In order to calculate the deadline for Opening Position Disclosures, it is therefore necessary to count forward 10 business days from the relevant date.

8.

Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service ("RIS").

9.

This Note is applicable if it says "See Note 9 below" by the name of the company in the Disclosure Table above.  The Executive has granted the company a dispensation from the requirements under the Code that announcements must be published via a RIS. The company is instead required to publish all announcements on its website. The Executive has also granted a dispensation from Note 3 on Rule 8 of the Code that disclosures made under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a RIS. Therefore, Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code may be made to the company at the address stated in the announcement that commenced the offer period.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1138 ET - A joint task force partnering the USDA and DOJ to investigate the high costs for farmer inputs like fertilizer and seeds is getting praise from groups like the National Farmers Union. "When just a handful of companies control the markets for seed, fertilizer and other farm supplies, they can raise prices at will," says the NFU in a note. "This leaves farmers squeezed between skyrocketing costs and low prices for their products." CBOT grain futures are mostly below the levels needed for farmers to turn a profit on their acres, and these farmers need both improved crop prices and lower input costs in order for these acres to turn positive. CBOT grains are mixed, with corn down 0.8%, soybeans up 0.1%, and wheat down 1.2%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1035 ET - Silver futures are pushing towards highs last seen in April 2011, according to data from FactSet. The most-active contract is now up 1.8% to $45.91 a troy ounce. While gold has dominated headlines, analysts are also looking at strong gains elsewhere. "It has been silver and platinum that have delivered the most eye-catching returns," says Ole Hansen of Saxo Bank in a note. Industrial demand has been a factor underpinning strength in silver, he adds, along with investors seeking the metal as an alternative to gold. "The metal's high-beta nature has amplified inflows from investors seeking leverage to gold's record-breaking run," says Hansen. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1018 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are lower heading into the expiration of the October contract following gains the previous three session. The market is seeing little support from the weather with a mild start to October likely to delay early-season heating demand. "Even as LNG begins to rise seasonally, the near-term fundamental outlook for natural gas is bearish as storage fills rapidly and weather remains mild," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. The October contract is off 2.8% at $2.823/mmBtu. Gas for November delivery is down 1.8% at $3.138/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1013 ET - Lean hog futures on the CME are up 1.8% in early trading, supported by yesterday's Hogs and Pigs report from the USDA. The data showed a decline in total U.S. hog and pigs inventory, running counter to expectations from analysts for a slightly larger inventory. "The Hogs and Pigs report is friendly," says ADM Investor Services in a note. "Actual numbers compared to estimates were just slightly lower." Live cattle futures are down 0.5% in early trading, with demand for year-end holidays like Thanksgiving and Christmas yet to materialize. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1011 ET - Oil futures are higher on rising geopolitical risk premium with continuing Ukraine attacks on Russian oil facilities,and President Trump's rekindling of tariff battles with duties on pharmaceuticals and heavy trucks. Although most of the buying has been by short-sided traders liquidating their positions, "the crude market has gained merit on steady demand seemingly keeping up with added supplies from OPEC+," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. The latest tariffs may signal to India and China "that additional tariffs could be heading their way if they remain buyers of Russian crude," he adds.WTI is up 0.8% at $65.48 a barrel and Brent gains 0.6% to $69.83. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1005 ET - The 25 basis point rate cut by the Federal Reserve, along with comments suggesting that more may soon come, propelled commodity futures to a strong month, particularly in precious and industrial metals, says Ole Hansen of Saxo Bank in a note. "The single most important tailwind for commodities this month has been the renewed easing cycle by the U.S. Federal Reserve," says Hansen. Expectations of more rate cuts before the year is out is buoying strength in commodities. But balancing out gains in metals is weakness in soft commodities like cocoa, coffee, and sugar, along with agricultural commodities like soybeans- where China's absence from the U.S. export market has been a pressure point. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0926 ET - CBOT grains are lower premarket with the approaching end of the quarter and the expected inflow of freshly-harvested crops coming from U.S. fields keeping a lid on prices. "Record U.S. crop could weigh on prices through harvest," says ADM Investor Services in a note, in reference to corn. The firm adds that fund traders remain net short on soybeans and soy products as well. The month and fiscal third quarter ends on Tuesday. Most-active CBOT corn futures are down 0.2% premarket, while soybeans are flat and wheat is off 0.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0804 ET - Silver prices continue to rally, with New York futures trading 2.4% higher to $44.94 a troy ounce in early afternoon trade after rising above $45 in the previous session. "The price of gold may have recently reached a new record high, but the price of silver has actually performed even more impressively," Thu Lan Nguyen, head of commodity strategy at Commerzbank, says. The precious metal has risen by nearly 56% this year, outperforming even gold, which has gained about 43%. "The record high price of gold is now deterring many and...alternatives are therefore being sought," she says. Meanwhile, platinum also rises, up 1.8% to $1,557.20 an ounce, its highest level since 2013. The white metal has surged 71% this year on expectations of another annual supply deficit. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0646 ET - Shares in European steel companies rise on Friday following reports that the EU Commission will impose 25% to 50% tariffs on Chinese steel and related products. The tariffs come as President Trump closes off the U.S. market, leading China to divert large quantities of goods to Europe and pushing down prices, German newspaper Handelsblatt first reported Thursday. Shares in German steel producers Thyssenkrupp and Salzgitter rise 1.8% and 4.1%, respectively, while Austrian peer Voestalpine rises 2.5%. Arcelormittal and Helsinki-headquartered Outokumpu both rise 1.7%. In Spain, Acerinox rises 1.7%. "This goes to show the U.S. might not be the only winner from tariffs - others are deploying the same strategy with foreign trading partners to protect domestic producers," AJ Bell's Russ Mould says in a market comment. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0614 ET - Palm oil fell, tracking lower soybean oil prices, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg. The narrowing price spread between the two competing oils likely weighed on market sentiment, Ng says. He sees support at 4,300 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,520 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for December fell 43 ringgit to 4,396 ringgit a ton. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0535 ET - Indonesia's trade deal with the European Union isn't expected to provide much of a boost to the country's long-term prospects, economists at Capital Economics write in a note. The agreement, signed earlier this week, offered a respite for the Indonesian government, which has been on the defensive with investors over its policy direction. The economists note that the EU will remove tariffs on more than 90% of Indonesian goods following the deal's implementation. Palm oil and other commodity exports stand to benefit, as well as the textile sector. However, CE cautions that Indonesia's palm oil industry still faces risks from the EU's deforestation regulation, which could significantly disrupt exports to the bloc. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0517 ET - Crude oil exports from Western Russia surged to a 10-month high this month driven by a sharp increase in Urals crude loadings amid intensifying Ukrainian drone strikes against key infrastructure, Kpler says. "Following these attacks, Russian crude oil runs are expected to slip to an annual low of below 5 million barrels a day in September," says senior market analyst Yui Torikata. "This forces producers to redirect crude that would have been processed domestically to the export market, with Kpler expecting seaborne crude oil exports to serve as a primary outlet for this displaced crude in 4Q." Exports have averaged around 2.5 million barrels a day in September, a level not reached since October 2024, and are projected to stay at about 2.25 million barrels a day if all scheduled loadings are completed by the end of the month, according to the data provider. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)
